title: Linear momentum and its conservation
paperName: Paper 1: Multiple Choice / Paper 2: AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Explore linear momentum as a vector quantity ($p=mv$), the fundamental principle of its conservation in closed systems, and its connection to force and impulse ($F\Delta t = \Delta p$). Differentiate between elastic and inelastic collisions.
learningObjectives: State and apply the principle of conservation of linear momentum to collisions and explosions.
Distinguish between elastic and inelastic collisions based on kinetic energy conservation.
Relate force to the rate of change of momentum and solve problems involving impulse.

simpleExplanation.title: Momentum: Motion's Staying Power
simpleExplanation.summary: Momentum is a measure of an object's motion, combining its mass and velocity. In any interaction within a closed system, the total momentum before is the same as the total momentum after.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine a game of snooker. Before the break, the total momentum of all the balls is zero. When the white ball strikes the pack, balls scatter in all directions. However, if you could add up the individual momentum vectors of every single ball, the total would still be zero. The momentum is simply redistributed among the balls; none is lost from the system.
steps: Momentum is a vector defined as mass times velocity, $p = mv$. Its direction is the same as the velocity's direction.
In a closed system (no external forces like friction), the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after. This is the principle of conservation of momentum.
In inelastic collisions, objects may deform or stick together, and kinetic energy is not conserved. However, momentum is still conserved.
Impulse is the change in momentum, $\Delta p$. It's equal to the average force multiplied by the time it acts, $F\Delta t$, and is represented by the area under a force-time graph.

flashcards.back: Linear momentum is the product of an object's mass and its velocity. It is a vector quantity. ($p = mv$)

flashcards.front: What are the SI units of momentum?
flashcards.back: kg m s⁻¹ (kilogram metre per second). It can also be expressed as N s (newton second).

flashcards.front: State the principle of conservation of linear momentum.
flashcards.back: For a system of interacting objects, the total momentum remains constant, provided no external resultant force acts on the system.

flashcards.front: What is a 'closed system' in the context of momentum?
flashcards.back: A system on which no external forces act. For example, the objects involved in a collision, ignoring external forces like friction or air resistance.

Impulse is the change in momentum of an object. It is also equal to the product of the average force acting on the object and the time interval for which the force acts. ($Impulse = \Delta p = F \Delta t$)

flashcards.front: How can you find impulse from a force-time graph?
flashcards.back: The impulse is equal to the area under the force-time graph.
A collision in which both total momentum and total kinetic energy are conserved.
A collision in which total momentum is conserved, but total kinetic energy is not. Some kinetic energy is converted to other forms (e.g., heat, sound).

flashcards.front: What is a perfectly inelastic collision?
flashcards.back: A collision where the objects stick together after impact and move with a common velocity. It involves the maximum possible loss of kinetic energy.

flashcards.front: Common Exam Trap: What must you always remember when calculating total momentum?
flashcards.back: Momentum is a vector. You must define a positive direction and use negative signs for any velocities (and thus momenta) in the opposite direction.

flashcards.front: How is Newton's Second Law related to momentum?
flashcards.back: Newton's Second Law can be stated as: 'The resultant force acting on an object is directly proportional to the rate of change of its momentum.' ($F = \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}$)

faq.q: What is the difference between momentum and kinetic energy?
faq.a: Momentum ($p=mv$) is a vector, while kinetic energy ($KE=\frac{1}{2}mv^2$) is a scalar. This means momentum calculations must account for direction. Also, momentum is always conserved in a closed system, whereas kinetic energy is only conserved in elastic collisions.

Yes. Momentum is only conserved in a closed system, which means a system with no external resultant force. If there is an external force like friction, air resistance, or a person pushing, the total momentum of the system will change.

faq.q: Can kinetic energy increase in an interaction?
faq.a: Yes, in an 'explosion'. For example, when a firework explodes, or a compressed spring is released between two trolleys. In these cases, stored potential energy (chemical, elastic) is converted into kinetic energy. Momentum is still conserved (if the initial momentum was zero, the vector sum of the final momenta will also be zero).

sections.content: Why does a tiny, fast-moving bullet have a similar impact to a slow-moving lorry? The answer lies in momentum. This concept is fundamental to understanding all interactions, from subatomic particles colliding in an accelerator to the orbital mechanics of planets. In this lesson, we will explore what momentum is, the powerful conservation law that governs it, and how it links to the forces that cause changes in motion.

Linear momentum is a measure of an object's 'quantity of motion'. It depends on two factors: how much 'stuff' is moving (mass) and how fast that 'stuff' is moving (velocity). Crucially, momentum is a vector quantity, meaning it has both a magnitude and a direction. The direction of the momentum is the same as the direction of the velocity.

items: p is momentum in kg m s⁻¹
v is velocity in m s⁻¹
The vector nature of momentum is critical in all calculations.

sections.content: The Principle of Conservation of Linear Momentum

This is one of the most fundamental principles in physics. It states that for any collision or interaction between objects in a closed system, the total momentum before the interaction is equal to the total momentum after the interaction. A closed system is one where no external resultant force (like friction or a push from outside) acts. Momentum can be transferred between objects, but the total amount in the system never changes.
$$\Sigma p_{\text{initial}} = \Sigma p_{\text{final}} \\ \text{For two objects:} \\ m_1u_1 + m_2u_2 = m_1v_1 + m_2v_2$$
Here, $u_1$ and $u_2$ are the initial velocities, and $v_1$ and $v_2$ are the final velocities of objects 1 and 2, respectively. Remember to use signs to indicate direction!

sections.question: A trolley of mass 2.0 kg moving at 5.0 m s⁻¹ collides head-on with a stationary trolley of mass 3.0 kg. After the collision, the 2.0 kg trolley moves in the opposite direction at 1.0 m s⁻¹. Calculate the velocity of the 3.0 kg trolley after the collision.
sections.solution:
Step 1: Define the system and the direction. The system is the two trolleys. Let the initial direction of the 2.0 kg trolley be positive.

Step 2: List the known quantities.
$m_1 = 2.0$ kg, $u_1 = +5.0$ m s⁻¹
$m_2 = 3.0$ kg, $u_2 = 0$ m s⁻¹
$v_1 = -1.0$ m s⁻¹ (note the negative sign for the change in direction)
$v_2 = ?$ 

Step 3: Apply the principle of conservation of momentum.
$\Sigma p_{\text{initial}} = \Sigma p_{\text{final}}$
$m_1u_1 + m_2u_2 = m_1v_1 + m_2v_2$

Step 4: Substitute values and solve for $v_2$.
$(2.0)(+5.0) + (3.0)(0) = (2.0)(-1.0) + (3.0)v_2$
$10.0 + 0 = -2.0 + 3.0v_2$
$12.0 = 3.0v_2$
$v_2 = \frac{12.0}{3.0} = +4.0$ m s⁻¹

The velocity of the 3.0 kg trolley is 4.0 m s⁻¹ in the original direction of the 2.0 kg trolley.

sections.content: While momentum is always conserved in a closed system, kinetic energy is not. This distinction allows us to classify collisions. In an **elastic collision**, total kinetic energy is also conserved. Think of collisions between snooker balls. In an **inelastic collision**, some kinetic energy is converted into other forms, such as heat, sound, and potential energy of deformation. Most real-world collisions, like a car crash, are inelastic. A **perfectly inelastic collision** is one where the objects stick together after impact, resulting in the maximum possible loss of kinetic energy.

items: **Elastic Collision**: Momentum conserved, Kinetic Energy conserved.
**Inelastic Collision**: Momentum conserved, Kinetic Energy is NOT conserved (it decreases).
**Explosion (reverse collision)**: Momentum conserved, Kinetic Energy is NOT conserved (it increases, converted from another form like chemical potential energy).

sections.question:
A bullet of mass 20 g is fired into a stationary block of wood of mass 480 g resting on a frictionless surface. The bullet becomes embedded in the block, and they move off together with a velocity of 10 m s⁻¹. 
(a) What type of collision is this? 
(b) Calculate the initial speed of the bullet. 
(c) Show that the collision is inelastic by calculating the kinetic energy before and after.
sections.solution:
(a) This is a perfectly inelastic collision because the two objects stick together after impact.

(b) Step 1: Convert mass to kg and define the system.
$m_{bullet} = 20$ g = 0.020 kg
$m_{block} = 480$ g = 0.480 kg
Let the initial direction of the bullet be positive.

Step 2: Apply conservation of momentum.
$m_1u_1 + m_2u_2 = (m_1 + m_2)v_{final}$
$(0.020)u_1 + (0.480)(0) = (0.020 + 0.480)(10)$
$0.020u_1 = (0.500)(10)$
$0.020u_1 = 5.0$
$u_1 = \frac{5.0}{0.020} = 250$ m s⁻¹
The initial speed of the bullet was 250 m s⁻¹.

(c) Step 3: Calculate initial and final kinetic energy.
$KE_{\text{initial}} = \frac{1}{2}m_1u_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}m_2u_2^2$
$KE_{\text{initial}} = \frac{1}{2}(0.020)(250)^2 + 0 = 625$ J

$KE_{\text{final}} = \frac{1}{2}(m_1+m_2)v_{final}^2$
$KE_{\text{final}} = \frac{1}{2}(0.500)(10)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.500)(100) = 25$ J

Since $KE_{\text{final}} (25 \text{ J}) < KE_{\text{initial}} (625 \text{ J})$, kinetic energy is not conserved, and the collision is inelastic.

sections.content: Impulse and its Relation to Force

How do we change an object's momentum? We apply a force. Newton's Second Law can be expressed in terms of momentum: the net force on an object is equal to the rate of change of its momentum. By rearranging this, we define a new quantity called impulse. Impulse is the product of the average force and the time it acts for, and it is equal to the change in momentum it produces. This is why airbags and crumple zones in cars are effective: by increasing the time of impact ($\Delta t$), they reduce the average force ($F$) for the same change in momentum ($\Delta p$), making the collision less dangerous.
$F = \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t} \quad \implies \quad F \Delta t = \Delta p = mv - mu$
Graphically, the impulse delivered to an object is the area under its force-time graph. This is a common exam question format.

In collision questions, always start by drawing simple 'before' and 'after' diagrams. Label the masses and velocities (with their signs!) on your diagrams. This is the single most effective technique to avoid sign errors and to correctly apply the conservation of momentum equation.

steps.caption: Momentum p = mv is a vector — direction matters.
steps.embedHint: Set masses and velocities — read momentum of each object before collision.

steps.caption: Total momentum is conserved in a closed system (no external impulse).
steps.embedHint: Run elastic collision — total p before = total p after.

steps.caption: Inelastic collisions: kinetic energy is not conserved; momentum still is.
steps.embedHint: Switch to inelastic — objects may stick; momentum still conserved.

steps.caption: Impulse FΔt = Δp links force-time graphs to momentum change.
steps.embedHint: Compare collision duration and force for the same momentum change.
